text: The surgeon uses a medium-large clip applier, docked to arm four, to retract the liver and gallbladder without directly grasping the gallbladder. The clip applier pushes the gallbladder and liver anteriorly, elevating the left lateral segment of the liver. The surgeon begins the 'open book' technique by opening the peritoneum on the medial side of the gallbladder, ensuring the liver is retracted to avoid interference in the cystic triangle area.

The surgeon uses the clipper bar tip to rest in the gallbladder fossa, carefully detaching the peritoneum and medial attachments of the gallbladder fundus from the liver to minimize liver laceration risk. The Firefly imaging system is used to check the anatomy, revealing no visible cystic duct or compound duct. The surgeon then rolls the gallbladder away from the liver, opening the peritoneum on the medial side to enhance exposure of the hepatocystic triangle.

The surgeon uses the open book technique to separate the gallbladder from the liver, focusing on the medial side. The cystic artery is visible traveling alongside the gallbladder, and efforts are made to preserve it against the liver side. The gallbladder is pulled further away from the liver, increasing safety by distancing it from the hepatocystic triangle.

The surgeon uses bipolar cautery to coagulate bleeding branches from the cystic artery near the gallbladder. The gallbladder is gradually pulled away from the liver, enhancing exposure of the hepatocystic triangle by opening the medial side of the gallbladder.

The surgeon carefully maneuvers close to the hepatocystic triangle, maintaining proximity to the gallbladder to avoid the right hepatic artery and duct. The gallbladder is pulled away from critical structures, allowing for a clear view and access to the cystic duct using minimal instrument exchange with the clip applier.

The surgeon continues to work high on the gallbladder, avoiding the common hepatic artery and ducts. The critical view of safety has been achieved, and the surgeon is dissecting more of the gallbladder while waiting for the clip applier to be reloaded. Using a hook, the surgeon attempts to identify a small bile duct, which turns out not to be one. The goal is to remove the gallbladder completely when the last clip is placed, while dissecting additional length on the cystic duct for clipping and division with cautery, ensuring no thermal injury occurs.

The surgeon leaves a portion of the gallbladder attached while preparing to apply a clip. The clip applier is positioned to avoid interference with the falciform ligament. The surgeon uses hooks to elevate the liver and continues removing gallbladder attachments while waiting for the clip applier to reload.

The surgeon uses a single clip applier to complete the dissection of the gallbladder, with the bedside assistant bringing in the third clip before the gallbladder is fully detached. The clip applier serves as a liver retractor, and the cystic duct is divided using the cut function with good tension. Firefly imaging is used to check for bile leaks and ensure no small ducts are missed. The hook is swapped for an endocatch through the umbilicus using the Hassan technique for gallbladder extraction. The clip applier also functions as a grasper to manipulate the endocatch.
